narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring your heart into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we could support—it's like piecing together a story from the chapters of your academic journey so far. At the top of that story is your Year 3 decile of 1.0, which stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward the possibility of not passing the bar, suggesting those final undergrad years didn't build the momentum we typically see in successful candidates. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 28.0, another heavy influence tipping the scales toward failure, as it falls short of the benchmarks that have historically paved the way for bar success. Your Year 1 decile of 3.0 added to this picture, reinforcing a pattern of mid-pack performance early on that the model weighed heavily. Layered in was your lower-middle family income bracket, which often correlates with added hurdles in the intense prep needed for the bar. Even your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.4 couldn't fully counterbalance these, as it nudged things further toward risk in the model's view. Your background as a white female and full-time student status played smaller but still notable roles, each subtly aligning with profiles that have struggled more often. Together, these elements painted a cohesive narrative for the model—a 57% chance of bar failure—that made us pause on your application, not because we doubt your potential, but because we've seen this combination trip up too many bright students before. It's tough, but this isn't the end of your story; many have rewritten theirs by targeting those standout areas like boosting test prep or seeking scholarships to ease financial pressures. You've got the drive—full-time commitment shows that—and with focused tweaks, you could shift this outlook dramatically. Hang in there; law needs voices like yours.
